subject: science

primary.justification: The chapter's central domain is the analysis of ecological relationships — between living organisms and their environments, and among organisms themselves. Every section builds the student's ability to trace patterns of dependence and response: biotic-abiotic interactions (organisms depending on air, water, sunlight, soil), biotic-biotic interactions (food chains, food webs, mutualism, commensalism, parasitism, competition), the cascading effects when one relationship is disrupted (frog depletion → pest rise → pesticide overuse), and the benefits and threats to ecosystems including the Sundarbans case study. C-3.3 (Analyses patterns of relationships between living organisms and their environments in terms of dependence on and response to each other) is the structural organising principle of the chapter from Section 12.1 through Section 12.9.2.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to analyse patterns of relationships between living organisms and their environments by tracing how biotic and abiotic components interact to form ecosystems, how feeding relationships and other inter-organism interactions maintain ecological balance, and how disruptions to these relationships create cascading consequences across the system.
